title: Java Versions: 8 → Latest LTS
objectives: Understand LTS vs feature releases via a real-life analogy
Memorize the killer feature of each LTS (8, 11, 17, 21)
Handle 'which version added X?' interview questions
Recognize preview features and how to use them

sections.markdown:
**Think of car model years.**

- **Feature releases** are like new model years — every 6 months, Java ships a new version (18, 19, 20, 22, 23…) with fresh features. Most teams skip these — they're too short-lived to invest a migration in.
- **LTS (Long-Term Support) releases** are like the model years the manufacturer commits to service for 8+ years — a solid workhorse you can plan a fleet around. Java's LTS versions are **8, 11, 17, and 21** — arriving roughly every 2–3 years. Almost every enterprise Java project targets an LTS.

**Interview reality:** interviewers absolutely love asking 'which version added X?' — because if you can answer, you clearly work with modern Java, not just Java 8 patterns from 2015. Anchor the timeline in your head:

> **8** = lambdas + streams (functional Java arrived) — 2014
> **11** = the first modern LTS, first fully-modular era — 2018
> **17** = records + sealed classes + switch expressions — 2021
> **21** = virtual threads + pattern matching + record patterns — 2023

Since Java 9, Oracle ships a **feature release every 6 months** and marks **one release every ~2 years as LTS** (Long-Term Support — commercial vendors like Oracle, Red Hat, Amazon Corretto, and Azul provide multi-year updates and security patches). Non-LTS releases still work, but they get replaced 6 months later.
## The LTS timeline at a glance

rows: Lambdas, Streams API, Optional, default methods, `java.time`, method references
`var` in lambda params, `HttpClient` (final), `String.strip`, single-file source runner, `Files.readString`
Sealed classes (final), records (final at 16), pattern matching for instanceof, text blocks, switch expressions (final at 14)
Virtual threads, pattern matching for switch, record patterns, sequenced collections, unnamed patterns/variables

sections.markdown: ## The 'which version added X?' cheatsheet

**The single most-asked interview question in this area:** 'When were virtual threads introduced?' Answer: **previewed in Java 19, finalized in Java 21**. Same shape for records (14 preview → 16 final), sealed (15 preview → 17 final), pattern-matching switch (17 preview → 21 final). Memorize the LTS-year anchors (8/11/17/21) and you can guess most feature dates.
## Sample program — features across versions
## Migration pain points

**Java 8 → 11 — the biggest jump.** Java 9 removed Java EE modules (JAXB, JAX-WS, Corba, activation) from the JDK. Apps depending on them break. Fix: add explicit dependencies (`javax.xml.bind:jaxb-api`, etc.). Also `sun.misc.Unsafe` and other internal APIs became inaccessible under JPMS.
**Java 11 → 17 — smoother.** Most breaking changes: removal of Nashorn JS engine (JDK 15), stricter access to internal APIs. Check with `jdeps --jdk-internals YourApp.jar`.

**Java 17 → 21 — mostly additive.** Very few removals. Main gotchas: deprecation of `sun.misc.Unsafe` (going away in future versions) and Security Manager finally removed. Virtual threads: watch for `synchronized` blocks — they pin virtual threads to platform threads.
**Common across all migrations:** third-party dependencies. Old versions of Spring, Hibernate, Lombok often don't work on newer JDKs. Upgrade deps first, then upgrade the JDK.
## Best practices
**1. Target an LTS.** For production apps, stick to 8/11/17/21. Non-LTS releases lose vendor support in 6 months.
**2. Move off Java 8.** Public updates from Oracle stopped years ago. Even Amazon Corretto 8 support has a sunset. Migrating to 17 or 21 is a real security win.
**3. Adopt new features gradually.** You don't need to refactor everything on Day 1 of a migration. Start with records for new DTOs, sealed for new state machines, pattern matching for new switches.
**4. Use `jdeps` before migrating.** `jdeps --jdk-internals` and `jdeps --generate-module-info` catch problems before they blow up in production.

**5. Enable preview features cautiously.** `--enable-preview` lets you use pre-final features (e.g. Java 20 previewed virtual threads before 21 finalized them). Great for hobby projects; too risky for production.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — Timeline flash cards.** Write a quiz to yourself: for each of the LTS versions (8/11/17/21), list the 3 most important features. Recite it.

**Why:** Interview reliability.

**Practice 2 (Medium) — Modernize a Java 8 snippet.** Take any Java 8 code — a stream + anonymous class + explicit types — and rewrite with `var`, records, and switch expressions.

**Why:** You'll see the readability lift firsthand.

**Practice 3 (Hard) — Run `jdeps`.** On a real Java 8 project, run `jdeps --jdk-internals` and note every deprecated/removed API. Draft a migration plan.

**Why:** This is exactly the exercise you'd do at work before a migration.

**Practice 4 (Expert) — Java 8 → 21 mini migration.** Take a small Java 8 project, upgrade to 21, replace: anonymous classes → lambdas, POJOs → records, if-else chains → pattern-matching switches, threads → virtual threads. Document each change.

**Why:** This is the exact interview story that impresses at senior levels: 'I led a Java 8 → 21 migration; here's what changed and why.'
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: Which Java versions are LTS and why does it matter?**

**A:** LTS (Long-Term Support) versions are **8, 11, 17, and 21**, released roughly every 2–3 years. Non-LTS feature releases ship every 6 months but lose vendor updates after 6 months. Enterprises target LTS versions because they get 8+ years of security patches from vendors (Oracle, Red Hat, Amazon Corretto, Azul Zulu, Adoptium/Eclipse Temurin). If a security CVE hits Java 22 (a non-LTS), your only fix is to upgrade to Java 23 or higher. On Java 17, you'll get a patched 17.x.y for years. That's why almost every production Java system runs on an LTS.

**Q2: Key features of Java 8?**

**A:** Java 8 (2014) transformed the language:
- **Lambdas** — anonymous functions as first-class values.
- **Streams API** — declarative data processing pipelines.
- **Optional** — model absent values without null.
- **Method references** — `String::length`.
- **Default methods** on interfaces — evolve interfaces without breaking implementers.
- **`java.time`** (JSR-310) — a proper date/time API replacing `Date`/`Calendar`.
- **Nashorn JS engine** (later removed in 15).

Java 8 is when Java became a functional-friendly language. It's also the version most legacy apps still run on, which is why interviewers love asking about it.

**Q3: Key features of Java 11?**

**A:** Java 11 (2018) was the first modern LTS after 8:
- **`var`** for local type inference (arrived in 10; Java 11 allowed it in lambda parameters).
- **`HttpClient`** finalized — a proper built-in HTTP/2 client, replacing `HttpURLConnection`.
- **`String.strip`, `.repeat`, `.lines`, `.isBlank`** — small but useful additions.
- **`Files.readString`, `writeString`** — simple file I/O without streams.
- **Single-file source runner** — `java MyProgram.java` (no compile step for one-file scripts).
- **JEP 320** — Java EE + CORBA modules removed. Biggest migration break from Java 8.

Java 11 marks the era of 'modern Java': modular, HTTP-native, and past the Java 8 removals.

**Q4: Key features of Java 17?**

**A:** Java 17 (2021) is the big 'modern LTS with expressive types':
- **Sealed classes** (final) — closed hierarchies with `permits`.
- **Records** (final at 16) — immutable data carriers.
- **Pattern matching for `instanceof`** (final at 16) — `if (o instanceof String s)`.
- **Text blocks** (final at 15) — multi-line strings with `"""`.
- **Switch expressions** (final at 14) — `switch` that returns a value.
- **Random number generator refactor** — new `RandomGenerator` interface.
- **Removal of the Applet API and Security Manager (deprecated).**

Java 17 makes Java feel a lot more like Kotlin/Scala for domain modeling — records + sealed + pattern matching = ADT. Almost every new-Java interview centers here or on 21.

**Q5: Key features of Java 21?**

**A:** Java 21 (2023) is the current LTS and the biggest jump since 8:
- **Virtual threads** (final) — massively cheap concurrency; write blocking code that scales.
- **Pattern matching for `switch`** (final) — dispatch by type, deconstruct records inline.
- **Record patterns** (final) — `case Point(int x, int y) ->`.
- **Sequenced collections** — `addFirst`, `addLast`, `reversed()` on List/Set/Map that have order.
- **Unnamed patterns and variables** (preview) — `_` when you don't care about a value.
- **String Templates** (preview) — safer interpolation.
- **Generational ZGC** (final) — much better throughput on huge heaps.

Java 21 finally answers 'Kotlin does X better' for many X: coroutines (via virtual threads), pattern matching, ADTs. It's the version to target for any new project in 2024+.

**Q6: What are the challenges migrating off Java 8?**

**A:** Three main categories: **(1) Removed APIs.** Java 9 removed the Java EE modules bundled with the JDK (JAXB, JAX-WS, activation, Corba). Apps that used them need explicit Maven/Gradle dependencies. **(2) Restricted internal APIs.** JPMS (Java 9+) locks down `sun.misc.*` — including `Unsafe`, which was widely abused. Fix: use the public replacements or `--add-opens`. **(3) Third-party dependencies.** Old Spring, Hibernate, Lombok, Groovy versions often break on newer JDKs. Rule: **upgrade dependencies first, THEN upgrade the JDK.** Use `jdeps --jdk-internals YourApp.jar` and `jdeps --generate-module-info` to find issues before they blow up. Once past Java 11, each further LTS jump (11→17, 17→21) is much smoother.

**Q7: What is the module system (JPMS) and did it succeed?**

**A:** JPMS (Java Platform Module System, aka Project Jigsaw) shipped in Java 9. It's a way to declare *modules* — units bigger than JARs — with explicit `requires` / `exports` in a `module-info.java`. **Goals:** reliable configuration, strong encapsulation (no more `sun.misc.Unsafe` from anywhere), scalable JDK (build custom runtime images with `jlink`). **Reality:** the JDK itself is modularized, and this delivered real benefits (smaller runtimes via `jlink`, no leaky internal APIs). But *application-level* adoption has been modest — most Spring/microservice apps still ship classpath-based, unnamed-module JARs. It's a success for JDK vendors and library authors, a mixed story for app teams. Still, understanding `module-info.java`, `requires`, `exports`, `opens`, `--add-opens`, `--add-reads` is essential for modern Java troubleshooting.

**Q8: What are preview features and how do you use them?**

**A:** Preview features are 'final-ish' language/API features that ship in a JDK release but need `--enable-preview` on the compiler and runtime to use. They're not experimental — they're feature-complete and expected to become permanent — but Oracle reserves the right to change them based on feedback. Records, sealed classes, pattern matching, and virtual threads all shipped as previews before being finalized. **Usage:** `javac --enable-preview --release 21 MyApp.java` and `java --enable-preview MyApp`. **When to use:** hobby projects, learning, giving early feedback. **When not to use:** production — you'll be forced to migrate when the feature finalizes or changes. Rule: use preview features to LEARN what's coming; use final features in production.

## Real-world usage

- **Most enterprise Java in 2026** — still 11 or 17, migrating to 21.
- **Modern Spring Boot 3.x** — requires Java 17+.
- **Android** — historically Java 8 (with backports); recent versions support up to Java 17 features via desugaring.
- **Big Data / Kafka / Cassandra** — moved off 8 slowly; most support 17.
- **Startups + new projects** — target 21 by default now.
- **Legacy TCS/Infosys/Accenture projects** — often still Java 8. Being able to migrate is a career-multiplier skill.

## Advanced notes

- **Release cadence** — 6 months between feature releases, ~2 years between LTS (currently 8→11→17→21→next LTS is 25 in Sept 2025).
- **Vendor distributions** — Oracle JDK (commercial for prod), OpenJDK (reference), Amazon Corretto, Azul Zulu, Eclipse Temurin (Adoptium), Red Hat OpenJDK, GraalVM. All compatible; differ in support, tuning, features.
- **`--enable-preview` vs incubator modules** — preview = final-almost, expect to migrate. Incubator = experimental API, expect to break.
- **Multi-release JARs (MRJARs)** — package version-specific class files (`META-INF/versions/17/`) so one JAR runs on multiple JDKs with tailored bytecode.
- **`jlink`, `jpackage`, `jshell`** — Java 9+ tools for custom runtimes, native installers, and REPL.
- **JVM options every senior should know** — `-Xmx`, `-Xms`, `-XX:+UseG1GC` / `-XX:+UseZGC`, `--enable-preview`, `--add-opens`.
- **Compatibility matrix** — Android Studio / Gradle / Spring Boot / Hibernate versions each require specific JDK ranges; check before upgrading.

flashcards.back: var, HttpClient, first LTS after 8; JPMS is fully in place.
Sealed classes + records + pattern-matching instanceof + text blocks.
Virtual threads + pattern-matching switch + record patterns + sequenced collections.
Final-almost feature guarded by --enable-preview; may change before finalization.

quiz.explain: Java 11 is the next LTS after 8.

options: Preview language features not yet final

challenges.text: Recite the LTS timeline and one headline feature of each: 8, 11, 17, 21.
Match each feature to its Java version: `var`, records, sealed, virtual threads, text blocks, switch expressions.
Rewrite a Java 8 sample using Java 21 features: records + switch expression + pattern matching.
Run `jdeps --jdk-internals` on any old JAR and list the problematic APIs.
Plan a Java 8 → 17 migration for a small project (Spring 5 → Spring 6, Hibernate 5 → 6, JDK 8 → 17).
Explain the JPMS module system to a colleague and show a `module-info.java` example.
Take a Java 8 codebase, run it on Java 21, adopt records/sealed/pattern-matching, and measure LOC before/after.

interviewQuestions: Which Java versions are LTS and why does it matter?
What are the challenges migrating off Java 8?
What is the module system (JPMS) and did it succeed?
What are preview features and how do you use them?
